sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Urinary catheterisation involves inserting a flexible tube (catheter) through the urethra into the bladder to drain urine.

### Indications
- Acute urinary retention (primary indication at mine site)
- Monitoring fluid balance in critically ill or unconscious patients
- Urinary incontinence management in palliative or immobile patients
- Prior to certain procedures

### Contraindications
- **Suspected urethral injury** — blood at meatus, perineal bruising, pelvic fracture → do NOT attempt; contact RFDS for guidance on suprapubic catheter
- **Known urethral stricture** — may need coude-tip catheter or suprapubic approach

sections.title: Equipment Required
sections.content:
### Standard IDC Pack Contents
- 14–16Fr Foley catheter (12Fr for paediatric, 20–22Fr 3-way for clot retention)
- 10 mL syringe + 10 mL sterile water (for balloon inflation)
- Lignocaine gel 2% (Instillagel) 10 mL — anaesthetic lubricant
- Urethral cleaning solution (chlorhexidine or saline)
- Sterile draping (fenestrated)
- Sterile gloves
- Kidney dish
- Drainage bag and tubing

### Catheter Sizing Guide
| Size | Use |
|---|---|
| 12Fr | Paediatric |
| 14Fr | Female adult; tight male urethra |
| 16Fr | Standard male adult |
| 18Fr | Standard male with larger urethra |
| 20–22Fr 3-way | Clot irrigation |

sections.title: Male Catheterisation
sections.content:
### Preparation
1. Explain procedure and obtain consent
2. Position supine with legs flat
3. Retract foreskin if present (replace after procedure — paraphimosis prevention)
4. Sterile field: open catheter pack onto sterile drape; place on trolley at bedside
5. Apply sterile gloves

### Procedure
1. **Clean glans penis** with chlorhexidine-soaked swab, clean in circular motion outward from meatus × 3
2. **Apply Instillagel:** insert syringe tip gently into meatus; instil entire 10 mL; apply gentle pressure to glans for 2–3 minutes (gel anaesthetises and lubricates urethra)
3. **Drape** with fenestrated sterile drape
4. Hold penis **perpendicular to body** (or slightly toward abdomen) to straighten urethra
5. **Insert catheter** gently with steady forward pressure — use dominant hand; hold catheter near tip
6. Advance catheter smoothly until **urine begins to flow** — at this point catheter is in bladder
7. **Advance to bifurcation** (full catheter length) before inflating balloon
8. **Inflate balloon:** instil 10 mL sterile water; withdraw syringe
9. **Gently pull back** until resistance is felt (balloon seated at bladder neck)
10. Connect to drainage bag
11. **Replace foreskin** over glans — paraphimosis if left retracted

### Resistance Points
- **External sphincter (mid-urethra):** ask patient to take a slow deep breath and bear down slightly — sphincter relaxes
- **Prostatic urethra (prostate level):** common in BPH — maintain gentle forward pressure; do not force; try a 14Fr; contact RFDS if unable to pass

sections.title: Female Catheterisation
sections.content:
### Anatomy
The female urethra is short (3–5 cm) and the meatus is located between the clitoris and vaginal opening. The meatus can be difficult to identify, especially in elderly women with atrophic changes.

### Procedure
1. Position patient supine with hips flexed and abducted (knees apart)
2. Sterile gloves, sterile field
3. Clean labia and urethral meatus from front to back with chlorhexidine swabs × 3
4. Apply Instillagel to catheter tip
5. **Identify meatus:** use non-dominant hand to gently spread labia minora — meatus is small, midline, anterior to vaginal opening
6. If meatus not visible: small amount of Instillagel at the presumed meatus; watch for gel entering correct opening
7. Insert catheter gently, advance 5–7 cm, until urine flows
8. Inflate balloon with 10 mL sterile water
9. Gently withdraw to resistance
10. Connect to drainage bag

### If Catheter Enters Vagina
- Leave misplaced catheter in vagina as a landmark
- Insert a second catheter into the correct meatus (now visible superior to the vaginal catheter)
- Remove vaginal catheter once correct placement confirmed

sections.title: Complications and Documentation
sections.content:
### Complications
| Complication | Recognition | Management |
|---|---|---|
| False passage | Resistance, no urine, patient pain | Stop; do NOT force; contact RFDS |
| Balloon inflation in urethra | Severe pain on inflation | Deflate immediately; advance further; re-inflate |
| Haematuria ex vacuo | Blood in urine after large residual drained | Usually self-limiting; monitor |
| Paraphimosis | Foreskin painful and swollen; cannot be reduced | Ice pack, manual reduction, urgent urology |
| UTI | Fever, cloudy urine, suprapubic discomfort | Catheter specimen urinalysis, antibiotics |

### Documentation
- Time of procedure
- Catheter size and balloon volume
- Residual volume drained
- Character of urine (clear, cloudy, blood-stained)
- Patient comfort post-procedure
- Urinalysis result
